sentences.text: Yes, I can.
My name is Teofil Kosinski.
I was born on the 1st of January, 1925, in Torun.
We were together, five children and my parents.
Two brothers-- with me, together, three boys-- and two girls.
My father was a rail-- railway worker, and my mother worked part [? jobs ?] from time to time because she has to feed and to work for the whole family.
Until-- until the begin of the war, I was going to the ordinary school.
I finished my second class of school also in Torun.
I was rather good pupil.
I had very good notes.
And especially in singing, I was very good.
My teacher said me, oh, you have a very good voice.
You must improve it.
Maybe later you will be some singer.
Then my family-- especially my father-- he told-- he said to my mother, we must do something with him.
He must finish high college.
He must be a mensch, as we call, somebody.
He must be somebody.
All of us are so simple.
My mother finished only four classes school-- father, also.
And the wish was of my parents that maybe I-- I will be a higher-- somebody-- that I will-- will be somebody.
So I wanted to go to the gymnasium-- or gymnasium.
I don't know-- English-- gymnasium.
I passed the examination for this gymnasium.
My mother ordered it by tailor a new uniform for this gymnasium, but I never used this-- I never wore this uniform-- maybe only for a picture once.
And I never begin-- began to go to school because on the 1st September, 1939 began the war.
I never forget this day.
It was rather cool day.
I was-- morning working with the German gardeners on the market because the salary, the income, of our family was not so big.
Everybody had to do something to help my parents.
So I was going always, before I went to the school, I worked on the market.
And, at this moment-- [? on ?] [? first ?] [INAUDIBLE]-- we heard signals.
And everybody said, war!
War.
War.
War.
Then I went home, and my mother says, yes.
It's very bad time.
Because she had very bad experience with the First World War.
But later, when we were talking about the war, my mother said, the First World War-- in compare with second war-- it was like dream because the second war was so bad as never before.
No.
I am a Catholic.
But I have many contacts with Jews.
We had no in Torun so many Jews.
But my mother was born in Golub-Dobrzyn.
In Golub-Dobrzyn, you have many of Jews.
She had sisters and mother-- my grandmother-- and my grandfather [INAUDIBLE].
So we spent always our, let's say, Christmas holidays or other.
It was not so far from Torun.
And first of all, we had free-of-charge tickets for the train because my father was working at the rail was-- a railway worker.
And there I have seen the Jews.
I like [? it-- ?] this was like in the movie, 'Anatevka.' Maybe in-- maybe it was-- do you know?
Did you see maybe this musical of Anatevka?
Small houses, yarmulke, the Jews, and the matzoh and everything.
It was so-- the atmosphere was so beautiful.
I liked go to-- especially to Dobrzyn.
My mother was worked as a-- when she was young, she was a-- how you say-- babysitter-- let's say babysitter.
And then for some Polish friends, even from in the school, they joked, oh, you look like a Jew.
Because me, I looked exactly like my mother.
And in-- in Torun, we had not so many Jews.
We had some restaurants-- Jewish restaurants.
We had some storehouses.
I remember that we made the shopping by the-- by [?
Leiser. ?] [?
Leiser-- ?] [? it ?] was very nice quality dealer.
And he gave us-- not at cash, even for a few months at the rate to pay it.
It was very good condition.
And the two boys in my school-- I don't know now the names-- they were also very good-- were very good friendship.
And I never forget when the war began.
Then-- when we have seen synagogue was burned and all books-- prayer books were burned.
And one of them, he said, you see?
Just now they started with us.
Maybe tomorrow they will start with you in the same way.
Was there a synagogue in Torun?
There was.
But whether the synagogue is still now, I don't know.
Because after the war, where I return to Poland-- later, you see-- my contact with Torun was very-- very seldom-- only from time to time.
So I didn't visit those places, even the places where I was arrested, where I have the very bad war experience, even this place where I met somebody-- I didn't go.
I didn't see the place.
So I don't know.
Oh, in my country was-- in my place in Torun, it was excellent.
As I told you, there were some Jewish restaurant.
Me, as a child-- because I had to work always during my holiday-- school holidays-- by German gardener or by Jew restaurants.
And I never forget-- for my job I [? had ?] received 50 groszy.
It was rather nice salary-- not salary-- nice tip.
But, besides this tip, I received wonderful bouillon with chicken.
It was so delicious.

And then, when I said my mother that I ate in this Jew restaurant, and she said, yes, the-- the bouillon is excellent because it is kosher poultry, and there is never dirty [INAUDIBLE] in [INAUDIBLE].
And therefore the taste [INAUDIBLE].
And in this restaurant they served only a half chicken with some pasta-- with some noodles, or macaroni as you say-- some-- nothing more.
And I never forget when I make the shopping [? dinner, ?] I was so keen not for this 50 groszy, but only for this bouillon soup.
It was not so very big.
Let's say maybe 60,000 of inhabitants, but we [? haved ?] our own theatre.
We had wonderful [? four ?] old church.
It was like-- we called it, like Krakow.
Torun was more Venezia [? in ?] Norden.
Thanks God Torun during the war wasn't-- not destroyed, not ruined-- so that everything original is until now.
Even the house where was born the very famous [? astronaus, ?] Mikolaj Kopernik-- or the German people and all over they say Nicolaus Copernicus in Latin.
And we say Mikolaj Kopernik.
Yes, there were also, but I never went to them.
First of all, I was a child.
But I was working by German gardener.
It was-- maybe he is not alive.
I can mention his name now.
He was [?
Bartel. ?] I never forget this name.
Very good guy.
He paid me also with some Polish coins.
But usually I received in a bag plenty of vegetables so that I could take this to my mother.
And he was great help for her.
She never went to the market.
She [? need'nt-- ?] she didn't-- she need-- she had not in the [? amount ?] to buy because I brought this.
I received as a compensation for my [INAUDIBLE], for my work there in the garden or in the market with him.
And it was maybe very good opportunity because by this gardener, I learned a little German.
And especially because my mother finished only German-- four classes German school-- my father, also.
Sometimes they wanted to say amongst them something secretly, and they spoke German.
And I was very angry that I couldn't understand.
Therefore ?] I [? insistent ?] to learn so quickly, and there would be not so secrets for me because I-- I could speak a little also German.
Oh, yes.
Our relation was wonderful.
We had not so big income, but everybody did something, and everybody tried to bring home-- even me.
When I received some sweets from my gardener or from this Jew lady, this owner of restaurant, I never-- never ate alone.
I brought home, and my mother share it to everybody.
And my father, also receive-- I never forget-- sometimes he receive some tips.
He brought some sprats, smoked sprats, a case of them-- she [? divide ?] so that we-- I have the best memory from my home.
And I was educated from my mother in the honest way.
She said always, don't lie.
Who lies, [? steal. ?] And you must be honest.
I have the best experience, the best memories from my childhood.
Oh, you see, it was only the tailor give us in very good-- very good rates for-- to pay for this uniform-- I don't know exactly, maybe one year.
And then my mother said, you will never use it.
Take this.
Give him again.
We never pay him.
It's better give him.
Later, when the war began, I complained a little my mother.
You see? ?] Maybe I could raise [? it. ?] Because during the war, there was no food, no dressing, no nothing.
And I would like to keep this uniform.
And I gave this to the tailor.
But then when the war began, he said, oh, boy.
Take this without money because even me, I cannot use it.
The money was without value.
Oh, you know.
The German people were-- we called them [? overhuman. ?] Polack and the Jews, they called-- we are not menschen.
We are not people.
We are-- they treated like animals.
We had a special rate of food.
No schools.
Everybody from young people was sent to the forced labor camp in Germany.
And for me, it was-- it should be very, very bad, because my mother-- no-- because my father, on the [? begin ?] of the war was sent to Warsaw to fight as a-- to fight against enemies.
And we were together with my family in Torun.
And on the [? begin ?] of the war, we were living in very nice railroad block with three rooms, with kitchen.
But when the German came, we had to leave this flat in-- in few hours.
That ?] what you could take [? for ?] only this very important.
My mother cried and asked to give permission for this.
No, because here are coming the Germans.
They will live here.
And then we are sent to very bad-- one room with a small kitchen, dark kitchen.
There was the water not in the kitchen.
The water was outside.
The bathroom-- no bathroom.
It was only toilet [? out. ?] Terrible.
It is unbelief to explain.
It should be shown.
How-- And you can-- just now, in one room-- we couldn't take the furnish from our room because there should be no-- there would be not a place for it.
But what we had to do?
We were lucky that we are together and are still alive because some bombs, especially on 3rd September-- it was Sunday-- few bombs were falling in some houses.
And some peoples were killed too, but we were lucky that we were alive.
Do you remember the day when-- when German troops came to Torun?
Yes, I remember.
But, you see, it was not so exactly when they came to Torun, only a little later-- because it was on Sunday on 3rd September, we had family in village.
And they came to us, to take us to the village they wanted to protect because everybody thought that the war is blitzkrieg.
It means it will be very short-- finished-- very short.
And we went to-- they came with a special horse cart, and they took us.
It was about 20 kilometers from Torun.
I know exactly the name.
It is Osiek-- Osiek in the direction by Lipno.
And we went there, but we heard that the German soldiers occupied [? somebody's ?] many flats, some rooms, your houses.
And my mother said to me and my older brother, please go to the place and look what's happening there.
We went there.
Our house was still standing.
This block was still standing.
We returned to the village.
And we said to my mother, come on.
We'll return there.
Nothing's [? so ?] wrong.
Because even on the [? begin ?] of the war, the German soldiers were very kind.
They gave soups.
They gave bread to the Polish people.
And I said to my mother, please come on.
There is not so danger.
And we returned, but without Father.
And, as I told you, we were not living long in this block.
We were moved-- we had to [? moved ?] according to the-- because there came two German.
They give us [NON-ENGLISH]-- [INAUDIBLE] how to say this-- this order that we must leave.
And we were moved to this very bad condition flat.
Yes, I know.
This time I cannot forget.
I-- I was 14 years old when began the war.
And even when we were in the village, I was [? always ?] 14 years.
And, as I told you, I was very, very anxious that maybe I will send also to the forced camp in Germany.
So I wanted to find a job-- but where?
Where to find?
But, thanks God, my brother, he was working also by one German bakery.
He didn't finish exactly his learning, his pupil for bakery, but he had some connection, good relations with the bakery.
He came home and told me that he knew about one job as a delivery boy in bicycle-- bicycle is good?
In bicycle.
And I went there.
It was [?
Max ?] [?
Gluck. ?] Maybe he is not alive.
He was German.
He was rather a fat, old man.
And his young woman, she was blonde hair and was nice looking.
I came there.
I started to ask him, in my broken German, for a job.
He asked me for the name.

First of all, if I can-- rode by bicycle-- because if I will work at his bakery, I have to take a special basket on my back and to bring the brot-- the bread-- some goods from his bakery-- to hospitals, to clinics, to some places of German families.
And it should be in very hurry way.
I couldn't go by foot.
He looked at me.
He asked me about my relation.
He wanted to know about my family.
I told him that my father is not home, only we children.
My mother has problems to feed so many children.
And then he asked his wife, and she said to him, oh, Max.
Take him!
He's so nice looking boy.
But first, she asked me about my name.
I said, this [? and that. ?] Oh, I like this name very-- and [? since ?] this time, I was working there as a delivery boy at this bakery.
Oh, you see.
Of course I would like to school-- to go to the school.
But during the war, in the begin, we're thinking only on the food.
Food.
Food.
I was always hungry.
In the shops, there was no food.
And the ration of food for Polish people was so little.
And I was in this age where I'm growing.
I was growing up.
I needed food.
And my mother couldn't-- from where?
But, thanks God, by this baker, he gave me some bread.
He-- sometimes when he didn't sell, take this to your mother.
Your mother has too many children.
There's this one or this one.
And I appreciated it very much because it was a great help for my family.
Oh, yes.
Even my-- from school, my very good friend.
His name was Hendrik M.
He was sent to the forced camp in [INAUDIBLE], in Germany.
He wrote-- on the begin when the post was going rather in good way, I received some letter.
I have probably even in Warsaw one picture of him, and he wrote me-- be carefully.
Stay so long as you can because this work condition, what we have here, is so terrible.
We have to wear our P, as a Polish, on this place.
And we are treated so bad.
He couldn't go ever-- only work, work, and work.
Maybe he is alive.
Maybe he would be not agreed that I have mentioned his name.
You see, first I had to ask him in [INAUDIBLE].
I give only Hendrik M.
The M is the other name, yeah?
If he-- if I knew that he's no more alive, I could.
But he was in my age.
If I am alive, maybe he is also alive.
You see, they-- when I had this good job, it was rather a good job because it was-- [? couldn't pay-- ?] it was joined with the food because the money-- reichsmark-- this was nothing.
Even-- I have received probably five mark, reichsmark, by this baker.
This was nothing.
But it was enough because I couldn't go to see the cinemas.
There were cinemas for German people only.
I couldn't go to the theatre.
Theatre was-- as I told you, I liked music very much.
And it was boring time.
You must understand.
I was 14 years boy, and I expected something more from [INAUDIBLE], not only darkness in the city.
We had to stay home from 8 o'clock evening until the morning 6 o'clock.
We couldn't walk.
Let's say it was summer.
To go to the bed at 8, it was too early.
So we're-- we were sitting before the houses.
We couldn't go on the street.
And so, only to breathe the fresh air.
But it was very boring.
It was really, for this time, for young people, extremely hard life.
I never heard.
I never seen-- you know, immediately after the war, from time to time I tried go-- before my-- before I finded this job by bakery, I went by foot to Aleksandrowo.
It is someplace.
There were many Jews.
I have seen them.
They were, with the [INAUDIBLE], walking on the street.
And [? there ?] was still free the bread.
We could buy free without stamps.
And sometimes I-- I-- I bought the bread for my mother.
But it was very short time, and then we couldn't never cross this frontier because Aleksandrowo belonged more to the district of Warsaw.
You must understand that Torun-- they [? tooked ?] as a part of [?
Reichsdeutschland. ?] Because from Berlin to Gdansk, there were-- they called this like corridor.
And many trains, like Mitropa with the German railcars, they crossed Torun.
I remember as a child I-- I went-- I shouted some German-- they gave me-- they throw the windows-- some sweets, some bonbons.
But, you see, maybe I will tell you this time when I was working by this bakery.
I was very glad, and I wanted to keep this job as so long I can.
And one time when I was delivering the bread, I crossed the German theatre in Torun.
I have seen their displays.
I liked to show-- to see the displays, how looked the-- the actors.
And once I have seen there was written some-- it was written exactly so-- 'Chorus singers wanted.' First, I thought, maybe the German only.
Maybe Poles.
Should be very good for me.
And then I started to go to this theatre and ask, in my broken German, whether they employed also Polish people, Poles.
They look at me-- because I was at this time 16 or 15 years.
Let's say 16 years.
And my voice was still change.
I had no real voice.
It was mutation, as we [? say. ?]
And they say yes, the Poles can also work at this theater, but under special task.
It must be combined together with other job.
Where are you working now?
I work still at the bakery.
And if you agree to join, to combine this-- whether [? it's ?] morning or [? it ?] [? is ?] afternoon-- because afternoon we have some examination, some lesson for singing.
And evening, there are spectacles.
Yes, I said, of course.
But evening, we cannot go out.
Don't worry.
You will receive a special pass-- this is pass for night-- night pass, let's say.
And with this pass you can be on the street, but only to the 12 o'clock so that if the performance is finished, let's say 11:00, you must find the time, and you must be home at 12 o'clock.
Of course, I wanted to do this because my childhood during the war was so boring.
And I went to my mother, and I said that I will work in theatre.
My mother was not so satisfied because she told me, oh.
You'll work double.
Morning here and evening there.
And when you will sleep?
I said to her, don't worry.
But in theatre, I have seen so nice people, and I like singing, Mother.
My brother, when I told him, he was disappointed and he didn't [? want ?] agree.
And he said, you see, in German theatre, you Polish.
You will go to work.
But I explained him, believe me.
I like music.
You must understand me.
Maybe I will improve my voice.
Maybe later after the war I can be a singer, professional.
OK.
He agreed with this.
And so I have to pass my examination.
I never forget this day, when I went there.
